region_of_origin: South India; widely served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Mutton Pepper Fry; FOODON_03315595 (mutton dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers cooked sheep/mutton meat; no specific code exists for the South Indian pepper-fry preparation. Used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Mutton Pepper Fry as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Pepper Fry as a side dish is approximately 150 g, equivalent to roughly 6–8 bite-sized pieces of cooked mutton.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Pepper Fry — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Pepper Fry (cr_id G00010261). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton (NDB 17175) as the closest approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Pepper Fry. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed meat-and-spice dish with low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g). The carbohydrate component derives primarily from onion and spice paste, which have a low GI profile consistent with values in the University of Sydney GI Database for similar low-carbohydrate meat dishes. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 11.16 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per meal and request reduced-salt preparation, as a standard 150 g serving delivers approximately 700 mg sodium, representing nearly 30% of the 2400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g) and high protein (14.05 g/100g) support glycaemic stability, but total fat at 11.58 g/100g warrants portion awareness
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Pepper Fry is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its low carbohydrate load and high protein content; however, frequent consumption of the high fat content may impair insulin sensitivity over time, so pairing with non-starchy vegetables and avoiding refined carbohydrate sides is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (467 mg/100g), saturated fat (~4.5 g/100g estimated), and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume Mutton Pepper Fry only occasionally and in small portions (≤100 g), as the combination of high sodium, saturated fat, and dietary cholesterol from red meat can raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; preparation with minimal oil and reduced salt is strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is a red meat high in arachidonic acid, which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to RA; high sodium further worsens tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise red meat consumption including mutton; if consumed, request preparation with olive oil to reduce the inflammatory fat profile and limit portion size to control sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Pepper Fry is a South Indian dry-fried dish popular in Singapore's Indian food culture, consisting of bite-sized chunks of mutton (goat or sheep meat) marinated and cooked with freshly cracked black pepper, onions, curry leaves, ginger, garlic, turmeric, and chilli in a hot wok or pan until the spices coat the meat in a dry, aromatic crust. The dish is characterised by its bold, peppery heat, slightly charred edges, and tender interior, with a deeply savoury and mildly spiced flavour profile. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice, roti, or flatbreads at lunch and dinner in South Indian restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 467 mg. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance, while the low carbohydrate load makes it suitable for glycaemic management in moderate portions. However, the elevated sodium and saturated fat from red meat raise concerns for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Black pepper contributes piperine, which has minor anti-inflammatory and bioavailability-enhancing properties, though this does not offset the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid profile of red meat for rheumatoid arthritis patients. The dish is halal and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton pepper fry?
Is mutton pepper fry suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat mutton pepper fry if I have high blood pressure?
Is mutton pepper fry gluten free?
South Indian mutton pepper fry Singapore

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010261, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy as the closest approved secondary source and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which is the approved GI source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific South Indian mutton pepper fry preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010261) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton (NDB 17175) as the closest approved secondary source; this is an approximation and may vary with preparation method and cut. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Pepper Fry was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros are non-zero and sourced from HPB. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (14.05), fat_g (11.58), and carbohydrate_g (7.44) are all non-zero.
